fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that auth handoff flow we talked about in the last sprint — the one where a third-party app can get a short-lived code on behalf of a user without us ever handing them the user's password. Basically it's the redirect-based handshake thing. The tricky part (and where we got burned last time on the login module) is knowing WHEN to throw an error directly vs. when to bounce the user back to the app with the error in the URL — we need to get that split exactly right or clients will have no idea what went wrong. There are also some config toggles the ops team needs to be able to flip — things like whether we force that anti-forgery value, whether we're strict about the callback URL matching, and optionally enabling that identity assertion feature for our SSO partners. Scope handling needs to work too — if someone sets up the deployment without a fallback scope, the request should fail gracefully. We also need the issued codes to be one-time-use and redeemable at a separate token endpoint. The output format for the runner needs to be totally deterministic — no raw secrets in logs ever. Please separate the concerns properly, don't just dump everything into one file like we did on the billing service. Refer to how we structured the redirect reconstruction piece for the URL component handling.

One more pass with the details the team asked for, since the main thing here is still getting the error split right. If the client cannot be identified at all, meaning missing or unregistered client_id, or if we cannot establish any safe redirect target, we do not bounce anything back to an app. That direct-response bucket includes no URI stored and none supplied, fragment in supplied URI, URI mismatch against registered, and multiple registered but none selected. In those cases the endpoint returns a direct HTTP 400 body response with the error. For all the other failures, once we already have a validated redirect target, we do a 302 back to that target and put error and error_description in the query string. That redirect bucket covers bad/missing response_type, missing state when enforced, invalid/missing scope, and user denied access.

For the client lookup edge cases specifically, both of the no-client cases are direct responses and never redirects. The response is HTTP 400, type=response, body.error=invalid_client. The only difference is body.error_description: use 'No client id supplied' when the client_id parameter is absent entirely, and use 'The client id supplied is invalid' when a client_id is present but not found in the registered catalog.

Also, the fixed client catalog should be treated as exactly four entries: (1) 'Test Client ID' — secret 'TestSecret', no registered redirect URI; (2) 'Test Client ID with Redirect Uri' — secret 'TestSecret2', one registered target 'http://brentertainment.com'; (3) 'Test Client ID with Multiple Redirect Uris' — secret 'TestSecret3', two targets 'http://brentertainment.com' and 'http://morehazards.com'; (4) 'Test Client ID with Redirect Uri Parts' — secret 'TestSecret4', one target 'http://user:pass@brentertainment.com:2222/authorize/cb?auth_type=oauth&test=true'. Any other client_id is treated as unregistered.

On config defaults, if the config object is omitted entirely from input, assume enforce_state=true, require_exact_redirect_uri=true, allow_implicit=false, use_openid_connect=false, and issuer is unset. That means state is required by default, exact redirect matching is on by default, implicit flow is not allowed by default, and the identity-connect stuff is off unless someone explicitly turns it on.

For redirect matching, there are two modes controlled by require_exact_redirect_uri (default: true). In exact mode, the supplied redirect_uri must match the registered one character-for-character including query string. In relaxed mode with require_exact_redirect_uri: false, the supplied URI only needs to share the registered URI as a prefix, so extra query parameters in the supplied URI are accepted. If the supplied URI fails matching in exact mode, return a direct HTTP 400 error with body.error=redirect_uri_mismatch, body.error_description='The redirect URI provided is missing or does not match', and body.error_uri='http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2'. This is the same redirect-URI validation behavior we called out for feature 2, especially features 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, including the missing-selection cases when there are multiple registered URIs and none was selected.

For the denied-user path, if is_authorized=false on an otherwise-valid authorization request — valid client, valid redirect, valid response_type, and state and scope checks all passed — then we still redirect 302 to the validated target, but with query.error=access_denied, query.error_description='The user denied access to your application', and query.state=<echoed state value if present>. No grant code is issued in that case.

On the rendering/output side, keep it totally deterministic. Each step produces one block in stdout. Blocks are separated by exactly one blank line, there is no blank line before the first block, exactly one blank line between consecutive blocks, and the entire output stream ends with a trailing newline. Each block starts with response=<step_index> using 0-based indexing, then status=<http_status>, then either type=redirect or type=response. For type=response, body lines are sorted by key. For type=redirect, always emit all six URL components even if they are empty: redirect.scheme=, redirect.user=, redirect.host=, redirect.port=, redirect.path=, redirect.fragment=. Pre-existing query parameters from the redirect_uri need to be preserved and merged with protocol-appended parameters like code, state, error, etc. In the final redirect output, all query parameters are written as query.<key>=<value> lines sorted alphabetically by key, and multi-valued parameters are comma-joined. That should follow the same redirect rendering behavior already shown in the Execution Contract and in feature6_1_redirect_reconstruction.json.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: authorize(request: AuthorizationRequest, is_authorized: bool, config: EndpointConfig, scope_util: ScopeUtil | None) -> AuthorizationResponse

trigger_keywords: fragment in redirect URI
what if the callback URL has a hash
URL with fragment component rejection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the supplied redirect_uri contains a fragment component (i.e., a '#' character followed by a fragment), the request is rejected immediately with HTTP 400, type=response, body.error=invalid_uri, body.error_description='The redirect URI must not contain a fragment'. This is a direct error — no redirect occurs. This check happens before any redirect-target matching.

trigger_keywords: multiple redirect URIs registered
client has more than one callback URL
what happens when no redirect_uri supplied and client has multiple
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a client has more than one registered redirect target and the request omits the redirect_uri parameter, return HTTP 400 direct error: body.error=invalid_uri, body.error_description='A redirect URI must be supplied when multiple redirect URIs are registered', body.error_uri='http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2.3'. The caller must explicitly choose one of the registered targets.

trigger_keywords: anti-forgery state parameter enforcement
is state required
enforce_state config flag behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The enforce_state config flag defaults to true (state IS required by default). When true and the request omits the state parameter, the endpoint redirects 302 to the validated target with query.error=invalid_request and query.error_description='The state parameter is required'. When enforce_state is false, the state parameter is optional and its absence does not block code issuance.

trigger_keywords: scope validation behavior
what if no scope is sent and no default scope exists
supported_scopes and default_scope configuration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scope enforcement is driven by scope_util config: supported_scopes (array of accepted scope strings) and default_scope (a scope string or false). When default_scope is false and no scope is supplied in the request, redirect back with query.error=invalid_scope and query.error_description='This application requires you specify a scope parameter', plus the echoed query.state if present. When a valid scope is supplied that matches a supported scope, the request proceeds normally.

trigger_keywords: output format for secrets and tokens
how are issued codes rendered in output
deterministic output normalization for grant codes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All server-issued secrets are replaced with stable placeholders in stdout: grant codes render as 'CODE', access tokens as 'ACCESS_TOKEN', refresh tokens as 'REFRESH_TOKEN', identity tokens as 'ID_TOKEN'. The caller's state value, protocol error codes, error_description strings, and error_uri values are wire-format domain values and are preserved verbatim. Additionally, whenever a grant code appears in a redirect, the line issued.identity_token=present or issued.identity_token=absent must follow, indicating whether an identity assertion was bound to that code.

trigger_keywords: identity token binding
OpenID Connect scope handling
use_openid_connect flag and identity assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Identity token binding is controlled by two conditions: (1) config.use_openid_connect must be true, and (2) the request must include scope=openid. When both conditions are met and the authorization succeeds, an identity assertion is bound to the issued grant code and the output line issued.identity_token=present appears. In all other successful grant-code cases, issued.identity_token=absent appears. The config.issuer string provides the issuer label for the identity token.

trigger_keywords: token endpoint redemption
how to redeem authorization code
grant code exchange for access token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A grant code issued in an 'authorize' step can be redeemed in a subsequent 'token' step by setting action='token', grant_type='authorization_code', and using the literal '@AUTH_CODE@' as the code value — it is replaced at runtime with the real code from the preceding authorize step. On successful redemption, the token endpoint returns HTTP 200, type=response with body fields: body.access_token=ACCESS_TOKEN, body.expires_in=3600, body.refresh_token=REFRESH_TOKEN, body.scope= (empty if no scope), body.token_type=Bearer.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The state parameter — an opaque anti-CSRF token the client sends in the authorization request. Controlled by the enforce_state config flag (default true). When enforced and absent, a redirect error response is returned. When present and the request succeeds, the state value is echoed verbatim in the redirect query parameters as query.state=<value>.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that identity assertion feature for our SSO partners
context_pointers.actual_meaning: OpenID Connect identity token binding — enabled by config.use_openid_connect=true combined with scope=openid in the request. When both conditions are met and authorization succeeds, an identity token (rendered as ID_TOKEN) is bound to the issued grant code, and the output line issued.identity_token=present appears instead of issued.identity_token=absent.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in my notes — you'd need to check the actual codebase or the detailed PRD for the precise behavior. My understanding is limited to the high-level business flow: clients redirect users here, we validate everything, and hand back either a code or a structured error. For exact parameter names, matching rules, or output formatting details, please grep the source or check the test fixtures.

feature1_client_identification.json.description: An authorization request is rejected before any redirect can occur when the client cannot be identified. Submitting a request with no client identifier, or with an identifier that is not registered, yields a direct (non-redirect) error response carrying an HTTP client-error status and a stable error code distinguishing the two situations by description.

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=invalid_client
body.error_description=No client id supplied

response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=invalid_client
body.error_description=The client id supplied is invalid

feature2_1_no_redirect_uri.json.description: When a request names a registered client that has no stored redirect target and supplies none in the request, the endpoint cannot safely redirect and returns a direct error response with a client-error status and an invalid-URI error code.

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=invalid_uri
body.error_description=No redirect URI was supplied or stored

feature2_2_fragment_rejected.json.description: A supplied redirect target that contains a URL fragment component is rejected with a direct error response (client-error status, invalid-URI error code), because authorization parameters may not be appended to a fragment.

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=invalid_uri
body.error_description=The redirect URI must not contain a fragment

feature2_3_mismatch.json.description: When a client has exactly one registered redirect target and the request supplies a different one, the request is rejected with a direct error response carrying a client-error status, a mismatch error code, and a reference link to the relevant specification section.

request.client_id: Test Client ID with Redirect Uri

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=redirect_uri_mismatch
body.error_description=The redirect URI provided is missing or does not match
body.error_uri=http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2

feature2_4_multiple_requires_selection.j.description: When a client registers more than one redirect target and the request omits the redirect parameter, the endpoint cannot choose one automatically and returns a direct error response (client-error status, invalid-URI error code, spec reference) requiring the caller to specify which registered target to use.

request.client_id: Test Client ID with Multiple Redirect Uris

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=invalid_uri
body.error_description=A redirect URI must be supplied when multiple redirect URIs are registered
body.error_uri=http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2.3

feature2_5_exact_match_required.json.description: Under a strict exact-match policy, a supplied redirect target whose query string differs from the registered one (even if the scheme/host/path match) is rejected with a direct mismatch error response and a spec reference.

request.client_id: Test Client ID with Redirect Uri Parts

cases.expected_output:
response=0
status=400
type=response
body.error=redirect_uri_mismatch
body.error_description=The redirect URI provided is missing or does not match
body.error_uri=http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2

feature3_response_type.json.description: Once the client and redirect target are valid, a request that omits the response-type selector, or supplies an unrecognized one, is reported by REDIRECTING the user agent back to the validated redirect target with an error code and description encoded in the query string, rather than as a direct error body.

redirect.user=
redirect.host=adobe.com
redirect.port=
redirect.path=
redirect.fragment=

query.error_description=Invalid or missing response type

redirect.scheme=http
redirect.user=
redirect.host=adobe.com
redirect.port=

feature4_1_enforce_state.json.description: When the endpoint is configured to require an anti-forgery state value, a request for a grant code that omits it is reported by redirecting back to the validated target with an error code and description in the query string.

feature4_2_no_enforce_state.json.description: When state is NOT required, the same request that omits the state value succeeds: the user agent is redirected back to the target with a freshly issued grant code and no error, and no identity assertion is bound to the code.

feature5_scope_enforcement.json.description: When the deployment defines a set of supported scopes and no default scope, a grant-code request that omits the scope parameter is redirected back with a scope error, while the same request that names a supported scope succeeds with a redirect carrying a freshly issued grant code.

The second step references the code issued by the first step.

feature9_identity_token_binding.json.description: When the deployment enables identity-connect features and the request asks for the identity scope, a successful grant-code authorization additionally binds an identity assertion to the issued code, observable as the code carrying an attached identity token (in contrast to the default flow, where none is attached).
